creator.name: Sheker K
creator.expertise: Solutions architecture and pre-sales engineering — 10+ years building enterprise integration platforms across healthcare, insurance, and financial services
creator.background: I've spent years watching enterprise engineering teams burn cycles on repetitive bug fixes. Every sprint, developers pull GitHub Issues, manually trace through codebases, write fixes, run reviews, and create PRs — for problems that follow predictable patterns. I've seen how AI agents like CodeRabbit and GitHub Copilot save 4.2 hrs/dev/week on code review alone. I want to build the system that closes the full loop: from ticket to merged PR, with AI agents handling the grunt work while developers focus on architecture and design decisions.

purpose.whoYouHelp: Engineering teams at mid-to-large companies (50-500 developers) who process 200+ bug tickets per sprint and want to automate the ticket-to-fix pipeline without sacrificing code quality
purpose.problemYouSolve: Developers spend 30-40% of their time on routine bug fixes: reading tickets, tracing code paths, writing patches, running tests, and shepherding PRs through review. Most of these fixes follow predictable patterns — null checks, off-by-one errors, missing error handling, config mismatches — yet each one requires a full human loop from ticket to merge.
purpose.desiredTransformation: Engineering teams go from manually processing every bug ticket to having an AI agent pipeline that autonomously analyzes tickets, generates candidate fixes in a sandboxed environment, runs code review, and creates PRs — reducing routine bug fix time by 60% while maintaining code quality standards through automated review gates
purpose.whyYou: I've architected enterprise integration systems that process thousands of transactions per second. I understand both the domain (software engineering workflows) and the infrastructure (Docker sandboxes, CI/CD pipelines, GitHub APIs). More importantly, I've studied the OpenHands CodeActAgent architecture at the code level — I know exactly how event streams, context condensation, and skill loading work. This isn't a wrapper around an LLM; it's a proper multi-agent system built on proven patterns.

product.name: TicketForge
product.oneLiner: TicketForge turns GitHub Issues into tested, reviewed pull requests using a multi-agent AI pipeline.
product.howItWorks: When a bug ticket lands in GitHub Issues, TicketForge's content-researcher agent analyzes the ticket to extract the problem statement, affected files, and reproduction steps. The ai-agents-expert agent selects the optimal fix strategy. A CodeActAgent spins up a Docker sandbox, clones the repo, and generates a candidate fix using Claude's code generation capabilities. The code-review-specialist agent reviews the fix against the project's coding standards, checks for regressions, and validates test coverage. If the fix passes review, TicketForge creates a PR with the fix, test updates, and a detailed explanation linking back to the original ticket.
keyCapabilities: Automated ticket analysis — extracts problem, affected files, severity, and reproduction steps from GitHub Issues
Sandboxed code generation — CodeActAgent runs in Docker containers with full repo access, generates fixes with test updates
Multi-agent code review — specialized review agent checks style, logic, security, and regression risk before PR creation
Event stream architecture — full audit trail of every agent action, decision, and code change for compliance and debugging
Context condensation — handles large codebases by intelligently summarizing context when event history exceeds limits
product.marketDifferentiation: Unlike GitHub Copilot (code completion) or CodeRabbit (review only), TicketForge closes the full loop from ticket to PR. Unlike generic agent frameworks like LangChain or CrewAI, it's purpose-built for software engineering workflows with Docker sandboxing, git-native operations, and code review gates. It follows the OpenHands CodeActAgent pattern — the architecture that scored highest on SWE-bench for autonomous code generation.
product.magicMoment: A developer opens their GitHub notifications and finds a PR already created for a bug ticket they were about to work on. The PR has a clean fix, updated tests, and a review summary explaining exactly what was changed and why. They review it, approve it, and merge — a bug that would have taken 2 hours is resolved in 5 minutes of review time.

audience.primaryUser: Alex, 34, Senior Software Engineer and Tech Lead at a 200-person SaaS company. Manages a team of 8 developers. They process 40+ bug tickets per sprint across 3 microservices. Alex spends Monday mornings triaging tickets and assigning them to developers, knowing that most fixes are routine but still require 1-3 hours each. Alex wants to free the team for feature work and architectural improvements.
secondaryUsers: Engineering Managers who want to reduce mean-time-to-resolution (MTTR) for bug tickets and track agent-vs-human fix quality metrics
DevOps Engineers who need to integrate the agent pipeline into existing CI/CD workflows and manage Docker sandbox infrastructure
QA Engineers who want to validate that AI-generated fixes maintain test coverage and don't introduce regressions
audience.currentAlternatives: Manual developer workflow (read ticket → trace code → write fix → write tests → create PR → request review), GitHub Copilot (code completion only, doesn't close the loop), CodeRabbit (review only, doesn't generate fixes), Cursor/Windsurf (IDE-based, requires developer in the loop), generic LangChain/CrewAI agents (not purpose-built for code workflows, no sandboxing)
audience.frustrations: Copilot helps write code faster but doesn't understand the ticket context. CodeRabbit catches issues but only after a human writes the fix. LangChain agents can be wired up but require months of custom development, have no built-in sandboxing, and break on API changes. The real frustration is that 60% of bug fixes follow patterns an AI should handle autonomously — but no tool connects the full pipeline from ticket intake to merged PR.

business.initialGoal: 3 pilot teams within 90 days, processing 50+ tickets/month through the pipeline, with documented evidence of 4.2 hrs saved/dev/week matching the GitHub Copilot benchmark
business.sixMonthVision: 20 teams on paid plans, $25,000 MRR, recognized as the reference implementation for autonomous ticket-to-code pipelines. Presented at a major developer conference (GitHub Universe, KubeCon, or PyCon).
business.constraints: Building with Claude Code as the primary coding agent. Infrastructure budget: $1,000/month for Docker sandbox compute and Claude API calls. The system must run on a single server initially (no Kubernetes required for MVP). Must integrate with GitHub Issues and PRs via gh CLI — no custom GitHub App required for MVP.
business.goToMarket: Open-source the core agent pipeline (MIT license) to build community trust. Sell a managed SaaS version with team dashboards, quality analytics, and priority queue management. Target early adopters through developer communities (Hacker News, Reddit r/programming, Dev.to). Publish benchmarks comparing TicketForge fix quality against manual developer fixes.

feeling.brandPersonality: The reliable senior engineer who quietly handles the routine work so the team can focus on what matters. Competent, transparent, and never flashy. Think: a well-run CI/CD pipeline — you trust it because it shows its work and never surprises you.
feeling.toneOfVoice: Technical, precise, and confidence-building. Speaks in engineering terms the audience already uses. Never hype-y or marketing-speak. Example status: 'Fix generated for ISSUE-342: null check added at api/handlers.py:47, 2 tests updated, review passed with 0 warnings.' Example error: 'Could not generate fix for ISSUE-350: reproduction steps unclear, affected files span 4 services. Escalating to human developer with analysis notes.'

frontend.rationale: Dashboard for monitoring agent pipeline status, viewing fix quality metrics, and managing ticket queues. Next.js provides SSR for fast initial load and React ecosystem for interactive dashboards.

backend.rationale: The agent pipeline is Python-native — Claude API SDK, OpenHands patterns, Docker SDK, and GitHub CLI integration all run in Python. FastAPI provides async HTTP endpoints for webhook ingestion and dashboard API.

database.rationale: Stores ticket analysis results, agent event logs, fix quality metrics, and team configuration. PostgreSQL handles the relational data model (tickets → fixes → reviews → PRs) and supports JSONB for flexible event storage.

auth.rationale: Users authenticate with their GitHub accounts, which also grants the agent pipeline access to their repositories. No separate auth system needed — GitHub is both the identity provider and the integration target.

payments.rationale: Industry standard for SaaS subscriptions. Handles team billing, usage-based pricing for API calls, and self-serve plan management.
